source: Hall et al. 2012, Toxicol Pathol, 'Liver hypertrophy: a review of adaptive (adverse and non-adverse) changes'
statement: Hepatomegaly as a consequence of hepatocellular hypertrophy without histologic or clinical pathology evidence of liver toxicity is considered adaptive and non-adverse.
key_use: Supports expected reversibility for adaptive hepatocellular hypertrophy after removal of stimulus.

source: Michalopoulos & DeFrances 1997, Science, 'Liver regeneration'; Mehendale 2005, Toxicol Pathol, 'Tissue repair...'; Anand et al. 2003, Toxicology
statement: Rat liver repair is rapid after sublethal toxic injury; in one chloroform model injury peaked at 24 h and returned to normal after 48 h, while delayed repair at higher injury burdens led to progression and death.
key_use: Anchors rat-faster-than-dog liver recovery and supports threshold rather than linear severity scaling.

source: NTP Nonneoplastic Lesion Atlas 2024, Liver - Fatty Change
statement: Macrovesicular fatty change is generally readily reversible; microvesicular change more often reflects toxicity and can be slower/less predictable.
key_use: Supports short recovery windows for simple vacuolation/glycogen-type change, with longer windows for toxic microvesicular injury/phospholipidosis.

source: Ettlin et al. 2010, Toxicologic Pathology, 'Successful Drug Development Despite Adverse Preclinical Findings Part 2'; Greaves 2012 Respiratory Tract chapter
statement: Drug-induced phospholipidosis generally resolves after withdrawal but may take considerable time; pulmonary phospholipidosis changes are still visible several weeks after withdrawal across multiple species.
key_use: Supports longer 'possible' recovery windows for phospholipid-type vacuolation/macrophage accumulation than for simple hydropic/glycogen change.

source: Reasor 1984, Am J Pathol / PubMed snippet; McCloud et al. 1995, Toxicol Appl Pharmacol / PubMed snippet
statement: Rat alveolar macrophage phospholipidosis is reversible after termination of treatment; after 7 days chlorphentermine treatment there was marked reduction in cellular changes after 4 days recovery, though lamellar bodies persisted.
key_use: Supports fast early functional/morphologic improvement but slower complete resolution in lung macrophage accumulation.

source: Huisinga et al. 2020, Toxicol Pathol, thyroid FCHH workshop; Yano et al. 2025, Food Chem Toxicol snippet; Canzler et al. 2025, Arch Toxicol snippet
statement: Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia are common nonclinical findings; rat recovery examples show substantial reversion within 14 days in some enzyme-induction settings, but increased thyroid weight can still persist after 2 weeks in stronger direct/indirect thyroid toxicant models.
key_use: Supports rat thyroid adaptive windows centered on 2-6 weeks and slower windows for hyperplasia than for pure hypertrophy/colloid depletion.

source: NTP Nonneoplastic Lesion Atlas 2024, Kidney - Renal Tubule Regeneration
statement: Renal tubular regeneration occurs as a reparative response to previous degeneration/necrosis and is characterized by basophilia, karyomegaly and nuclear crowding.
key_use: Supports interpreting tubular basophilia as a healing lesion rather than a primary chronic lesion.

source: Cuppage & Tate 1967, Am J Pathol; Haagsma et al. 1980, Br J Exp Pathol snippets
statement: After mercuric chloride injury in rat kidney, tubular epithelium was reconstituted between days 2 and 5; permanent patchy fibrotic lesions can occur after more severe injury.
key_use: Supports very fast recovery for mild acute tubular injury in rats and a severity threshold beyond which chronic residual lesions remain.

source: Kiss et al. 2016, Histopathological Evaluation of Contrast-Induced AKI Rodent Models
statement: Tubular vacuolization is often reversible even after very high contrast media doses; in one rat study the vacuolization almost disappeared 48 hours after iobitridol.
key_use: Supports short windows for osmotic/hydropic vacuolation distinct from necrosis.

source: Mengs et al. 2004, 13-week senna rat study with 8-week recovery
statement: After 8 weeks recovery, histopathologic kidney abnormalities reversed except for residual brown pigment.
key_use: Supports separating reparative basophilia (recoverable) from pigment (can persist).

source: Everds et al. 2013, Toxicol Pathol, stress responses review
statement: Routine study stress can produce thymic/splenic lymphoid depletion, altered thymus/spleen/adrenal weights, and altered leukocyte counts.
key_use: Supports rapid reversibility expectations for stress-mediated lymphoid/adrenal findings once stimulus ends.

source: Branceni et al. 1966, thymic involution and recovery in prednisolone-treated neonatal rats
statement: After steroid-induced thymic involution in rats, thymic mass, thymocytes and peripheral lymphocytes recovered fully by 3 weeks.
key_use: Anchors rat thymic recovery windows around 1-3 weeks for stress/steroid-type involution.

source: Zhang et al. 2020, Sepin-1 28-day rat toxicity study with 28-day recovery
statement: Minimal-to-moderate splenic extramedullary hematopoiesis, minimal splenic lymphoid depletion, minimal-to-mild thymic lymphoid depletion, and minimal-to-mild lymph node hyperplasia were mostly reversible after a 28-day recovery period.
key_use: Supports 2-4 week windows for reactive hematolymphoid findings in rats.

source: Miyata et al. 2009, Evaluation of Myelotoxicity in Dietary Restricted Rats
statement: After 14 days dosing and 7 days recovery, reticulocyte and platelet rebound plus splenic EMH were observed in 5-FU-treated rats, indicating marrow recovery dynamics within about 1 week.
key_use: Supports short marrow rebound windows for proliferative hematopoietic tissues in rats.

source: Reagan et al. 2011 best practices for bone marrow; Hu et al. 2016 palbociclib; Graziano et al. 1997 CI-994
statement: Bone marrow suppression/hypocellularity can be reversible after withdrawal; in one rat/dog toxicity program all non-testicular changes reversed within 4 weeks in rats and 9 weeks in dogs.
key_use: Supports species-dependent rat-faster-than-dog modifiers for marrow lesions.

source: Vidal et al. 2015, Morphologic manifestations of testicular and epididymal toxicity; Soares et al. 2009 dog spermatogenic cycle
statement: Many spermatogonial injuries can be reversible if surviving stem spermatogonia remain; recovery is constrained by the long duration of spermatogenesis. Total spermatogenesis duration is ~40-54 days in most mammals and toward the upper end in dogs.
key_use: Supports lesion windows tied to at least one spermatogenic cycle and slower dog/NHP recovery.

source: Campion et al. 2012 male reprotoxicity review
statement: In a Leydig-cell toxicant model, signs of recovering spermatogenesis were present by 3 weeks post-exposure and mature Leydig cells by 5 weeks.
key_use: Supports rat recovery windows of several weeks, not days, for endocrine/testicular lesions.

source: Graziano et al. 1997, CI-994 rat/dog toxicity
statement: Testicular degeneration in rats and dogs was the notable lesion that did not fully reverse within 4-week rat and 9-week dog recovery periods, whereas other changes did.
key_use: Supports classifying marked testicular lesions/atrophy as slower or potentially non-recovering.

source: Nolte et al. 2016 GI INHAND; Greaves 2012 digestive system chapter
statement: Florid nonglandular stomach hyperplasia can be completely reversible following withdrawal of the inciting stimulus; in an omeprazole model functional and morphologic gastric changes after 60 days treatment were fully reversible after 42 days withdrawal.
key_use: Supports expected reversibility for reactive hyperplasia and multi-week healing for deeper ulcerative change.

source: Berridge et al. 2016 INHAND cardiovascular lesions
statement: Cardiomyocytes are terminally differentiated; cardiomyocyte necrosis represents permanent loss of contractile capability.
key_use: Supports 'none' or very limited reversibility for true cardiomyocyte necrosis and fibrosis regardless of grade.

source: Baldrick 2016 vaccine dose-site reactions; Green 2016 Preclinical Toxicology of Vaccines
statement: A non-dose recovery period of 2-4 weeks was sufficient to show reversibility of typical vaccine dose-site effects. Simple injection trauma shows reduced inflammation and healing with regeneration/fibrosis by day 10, but alum-associated macrophage aggregates/focal fibrosis may persist for months to 1 year.
key_use: Supports short windows for ordinary injection-site inflammation/necrosis, but long persistence for adjuvant granulomatous change and fibrosis.

source: Baldrick 2016; vaccine-related lymphoid findings
statement: Reactive draining lymph node and splenic changes accompanying vaccination generally reverse within a 2-4 week recovery interval.
key_use: Supports expected reversibility for reactive lymph node hyperplasia/histiocytosis when inciting antigen/material is cleared.

source: Baldrick 2016 / Green 2016
statement: Organ-weight recovery generally parallels the underlying lesion: thymus/spleen effects with immune/stress responses recover over weeks, while dose-site granulomatous changes can persist much longer.
key_use: Supports deriving organ-weight timelines from pathology rather than creating separate organ-agnostic constants.

source: Derelanko 1987 rat RBC lifespan; An 2016 physiology model; Heilmann 1993 dog platelet lifespan
statement: Rat RBC lifespan is ~55-66 days; dog platelet lifespan is ~6 days. These turnover differences support much faster platelet/reticulocyte normalization than RBC mass normalization, and slower dog than rat RBC recovery.
key_use: Supports continuous endpoint timelines for hematology and species modifiers.

source: OECD TG 407 (2008); OECD TG 408; EPA OPPTS 870.3100; ICH M3(R2)
statement: OECD 407 uses at least 14 days follow-up after 28-day dosing; OECD 408 uses an appropriate recovery period after 90-day dosing; EPA 90-day oral toxicity guidance allows a satellite group observed for reversibility/persistence; ICH M3(R2) frames reversibility as a goal of repeated-dose safety assessment.
key_use: Shows that common recovery-group durations are design conventions, not lesion-specific biology, and therefore should not be reused as lookup constants.

source: Schafer et al. 2018 severity grades; Miller 2017 cell injury review; NTP degeneration atlas
statement: Severity grades describe extent/complexity of morphologic change; severe or prolonged injury can pass a 'point of no return' into irreversible necrosis. No source supports a universal linear week-multiplier across organs.
key_use: Supports replacing flat multipliers with lesion-specific threshold rules.
